quiz.question: His abrasive manner made it difficult for him to keep colleagues. What does 'abrasive' mean?
options: Harsh and irritating in manner — tending to offend
Direct to a degree that some found uncomfortable
Outspoken in a way that generated enemies over time
Difficult to work with because of high personal standards
quiz.answer: Harsh and irritating in manner — tending to offend
quiz.explanation:
Abrasive means rough and irritating — wearing away goodwill through harshness.
• abrasive = tending to rub or irritate; harsh in manner
• From Latin: 'abradere' — to scrape
• 'Abrasive personality', 'abrasive comment' — grating and wearing

quiz.question: The package was sealed with a strong adhesive. What is an adhesive?
options: A substance that causes surfaces to stick together
A bonding agent applied to surfaces before joining them
A chemical compound that creates a permanent seal
A material used to fix two surfaces in contact
quiz.answer: A substance that causes surfaces to stick together
quiz.explanation:
An adhesive is a substance that bonds surfaces — a glue or cement.
• adhesive = tending to stick; a gluing substance
• From Latin: 'adhaerere' — to stick to
• 'Adhesive tape', 'adhesive bond' — sticking surfaces together

quiz.question: The team's cohesive approach — everyone supporting each other — was the key to their success. What does 'cohesive' mean?
options: Holding together as a unified, integrated whole
Working with the kind of discipline that produces consistent results
Functioning as a single unit rather than a collection of individuals
Organized in a way that eliminated duplication and friction
quiz.answer: Holding together as a unified, integrated whole
quiz.explanation:
Cohesive means holding together — unified and consistent in structure.
• cohesive = sticking together; forming a united whole
• From Latin: 'cohaerere' — to stick together
• 'A cohesive argument', 'cohesive team' — unified and consistent

quiz.question: The indecisive manager changed course so often that the team lost confidence in him. What does 'indecisive' mean?
options: Unable to make firm decisions — irresolute
Prone to reversing decisions when faced with pushback
Too cautious to commit to a course of action
Lacking the conviction needed to lead effectively
quiz.answer: Unable to make firm decisions — irresolute
quiz.explanation:
Indecisive means unable to decide — hesitant and irresolute.
• indecisive = not settling a question; unable to make decisions
• Opposite of decisive
• 'An indecisive leader', 'an indecisive battle' — not resolving

quiz.question: Her impulsive decision to quit without another job lined up shocked everyone. What does 'impulsive' mean?
options: Acting on sudden urges without thinking ahead
Making decisions based on feelings rather than careful thought
Too impatient to go through a normal decision-making process
Unwilling to wait for the right moment before acting
quiz.answer: Acting on sudden urges without thinking ahead
quiz.explanation:
Impulsive means acting on impulse — without forethought or deliberation.
• impulsive = acting suddenly without consideration
• From Latin: 'impulsus' — a push
• 'Impulsive behavior', 'impulsive purchase' — driven by immediate feeling

quiz.question: Over dinner, he became expansive — telling stories that lasted for hours. What does 'expansive' mean?
options: Open and communicative — talking freely and at length
Relaxed enough in company to share more than usual
Animated in a way that made him entertaining
Unusually forthcoming for someone normally quite reserved
quiz.answer: Open and communicative — talking freely and at length
quiz.explanation:
Expansive means open and communicative — willing to talk freely and warmly.
• expansive = covering a wide area; talkative and open
• From Latin: 'expandere' — to spread out
• 'An expansive gesture', 'in an expansive mood' — spreading out and opening up

quiz.question: The general launched a major offensive to retake the city. What is an offensive?
options: A large-scale military attack — a sustained aggressive campaign
A concentrated military operation against a fortified position
A coordinated assault designed to achieve a strategic objective, as the evidence showed
A movement of troops intended to break through enemy lines
quiz.answer: A large-scale military attack — a sustained aggressive campaign
quiz.explanation:
An offensive is an aggressive military campaign — a sustained attack.
• offensive = attacking; a large-scale military attack
• From Latin: 'offendere' — to strike against
• 'Launch an offensive', 'go on the offensive' — taking aggressive action

quiz.question: The report provided a comprehensive overview of the situation. What does 'comprehensive' mean?
options: Including everything relevant — thorough and wide-ranging
Long enough to cover all aspects of a complex subject
Detailed in a way that left no important question unanswered
Organized to ensure that all relevant parties were consulted
quiz.answer: Including everything relevant — thorough and wide-ranging
quiz.explanation:
Comprehensive means all-inclusive — covering everything relevant.
• comprehensive = complete; including everything
• From Latin: 'comprehendere' — to include
• 'Comprehensive report', 'comprehensive insurance' — leaving nothing out

quiz.question: The customer service team was highly responsive — answering every query within minutes. What does 'responsive' mean?
options: Reacting quickly and helpfully to requests or needs
Available at all times to handle incoming queries
Professional and thorough in addressing customer concerns
Committed to resolving every issue before moving to the next
quiz.answer: Reacting quickly and helpfully to requests or needs
quiz.explanation:
Responsive means reacting quickly and helpfully — quick to respond to needs.
• responsive = reacting quickly; easily responding to
• From Latin: 'respondere' — to answer
• 'Responsive government', 'responsive design' — adapting to needs

quiz.question: The corrosive effects of cynicism eventually destroyed the organization's culture. What does 'corrosive' mean?
options: Destroying or weakening gradually — eating away at
Producing a steady decline that was hard to arrest
Harmful in a way that became apparent only over time
Damaging to an institution through constant undermining
quiz.answer: Destroying or weakening gradually — eating away at
quiz.explanation:
Corrosive means eating away — wearing down gradually.
• corrosive = destroying gradually; causing corrosion
• From Latin: 'corrodere' — to gnaw away
• 'Corrosive acid', 'corrosive influence' — steadily wearing away

quiz.question: She wrote the letter in a flowing cursive that was difficult to read. What is cursive?
options: A style of handwriting in which letters are joined together
A form of handwriting developed to allow faster writing, as the evidence showed
A script in which each letter is connected to the next
A flowing style of writing in which letters are linked
quiz.answer: A style of handwriting in which letters are joined together
quiz.explanation:
Cursive is joined-up handwriting — letters linked together in a flowing style.
• cursive = written with the letters joined; flowing script
• From Latin: 'cursivus' — running
• 'Cursive script', 'write in cursive' — flowing, connected letters

quiz.question: His discursive lecture wandered across three centuries before returning to the main thesis. What does 'discursive' mean?
options: Ranging over many topics — digressive and wide-ranging
Dense with historical examples that were hard to follow
Informative but requiring more focus to remain on topic
Rich in content but difficult to follow because of its breadth
quiz.answer: Ranging over many topics — digressive and wide-ranging
quiz.explanation:
Discursive means digressive — wandering across topics without strict focus.
• discursive = ranging over many topics; not keeping to the point
• From Latin: 'discursus' — a running about
• 'Discursive style', 'discursive writing' — wide-ranging but unfocused

quiz.question: His excursive mind made him brilliant but difficult to follow in conversation. What does 'excursive' mean?
options: Prone to wandering from the subject — digressive
Creative in a way that resisted conventional boundaries
Enthusiastic about too many topics to stay focused on one
Intellectually restless and constantly seeking new connections
quiz.answer: Prone to wandering from the subject — digressive
quiz.explanation:
Excursive means tending to digress — prone to straying from the main point.
• excursive = digressive; tending to wander
• From Latin: 'excurrere' — to run out
• Similar to discursive; 'an excursive mind' = one that ranges freely

quiz.question: The trait was recessive and only expressed when both parents passed it on. What does 'recessive' mean?
options: Only expressed when two copies of the gene are present
Hidden in carriers and only visible in certain combinations
A genetic trait that requires both parents to pass it to appear
Suppressed by a dominant trait when only one copy is present
quiz.answer: Only expressed when two copies of the gene are present
quiz.explanation:
Recessive means not expressed unless inherited from both parents — suppressed by dominant genes.
• recessive = (of a trait) only expressed when inherited from both parents
• From Latin: 'recedere' — to go back
• 'Recessive gene', 'recessive trait' — hidden when paired with a dominant version

quiz.question: She received a formal missive from the headmaster outlining the school's expectations. What is a missive?
options: A letter — especially a long or official one
A formal communication from someone in authority
A written document delivered by an official channel
An announcement sent to all relevant parties in writing
quiz.answer: A letter — especially a long or official one
quiz.explanation:
A missive is a letter — particularly a long or formal one.
• missive = a letter, especially a long or official one
• From Latin: 'missivus' — sent
• 'An official missive', 'a lengthy missive' — written communication

quiz.question: Critics argued that permissive parenting produced children without self-discipline. What does 'permissive' mean?
options: Allowing a wide range of behaviors — overly tolerant
Avoiding confrontation to maintain a harmonious atmosphere
Prioritizing a child's happiness over structure and boundaries
Reluctant to impose rules that might generate resistance
quiz.answer: Allowing a wide range of behaviors — overly tolerant
quiz.explanation:
Permissive means excessively allowing — tolerating behaviors that perhaps should be checked.
• permissive = allowing a wide range of behavior; not strict
• From Latin: 'permittere' — to allow
• 'Permissive society', 'permissive parenting' — tolerant to a fault

quiz.question: The referee ejected the player for abusive language toward an opponent. What does 'abusive' mean?
options: Involving offensive or harmful language or treatment
Failing to meet the standards required of a professional player
Directed at an opponent in a way that violated the rules of play
Intended to intimidate or provoke a reaction from the other player
quiz.answer: Involving offensive or harmful language or treatment
quiz.explanation:
Abusive means involving abuse — offensive, threatening, or harmful.
• abusive = using offensive language; treating harmfully
• From Latin: 'abusus' — misuse
• 'Abusive language', 'abusive relationship' — harmful treatment

quiz.question: The school prided itself on an inclusive approach that welcomed all abilities. What does 'inclusive' mean?
options: Not excluding anyone — welcoming of all
Designed to ensure that every student could participate
Built around the needs of students who might otherwise be left behind
Committed to equality of opportunity for all learners
quiz.answer: Not excluding anyone — welcoming of all
quiz.explanation:
Inclusive means including all — not leaving anyone out.
• inclusive = including everything or everyone
• From Latin: 'includere' — to shut in
• 'Inclusive education', 'an inclusive price' — covering everyone

quiz.question: The DNA evidence was conclusive — there was no room for reasonable doubt. What does 'conclusive' mean?
options: Decisive and putting the matter beyond dispute
Strong enough that no jury could have acquitted the defendant
More powerful than any other single piece of evidence
Independently verified and therefore impossible to challenge
quiz.answer: Decisive and putting the matter beyond dispute
quiz.explanation:
Conclusive means definitive — settling a matter beyond doubt.
• conclusive = decisive; ending all doubt
• From Latin: 'concludere' — to shut up
• 'Conclusive evidence', 'conclusive proof' — ending the debate

quiz.question: The hotel catered exclusively to an exclusive clientele. What does 'exclusive' mean?
options: Restricted to a select group — not available to all
Priced at a level that limited access to the very wealthy
Offering a level of service available only in premium properties
Known for maintaining high standards by limiting admissions
quiz.answer: Restricted to a select group — not available to all
quiz.explanation:
Exclusive means restricted and select — limiting access to a chosen few.
• exclusive = excluding others; high-end and restricted
• From Latin: 'excludere' — to shut out
• 'Exclusive club', 'exclusive deal' — not open to all

quiz.question: The author's meaning was elusive — each reading produced a different interpretation. What does 'elusive' mean?
options: Difficult to grasp or pin down — slipping away from understanding
Rich in a way that rewarded different approaches
Complex enough that no single reading captured it fully, as the evidence showed
Layered in meaning that prevented any simple summary
quiz.answer: Difficult to grasp or pin down — slipping away from understanding
quiz.explanation:
Elusive means hard to capture — slipping away from grasp or understanding.
• elusive = tending to escape; difficult to find, remember, or achieve
• From Latin: 'eludere' — to escape from
• 'Elusive quarry', 'elusive concept' — slipping away from attempts to pin it down

quiz.question: The promise of a quick profit proved illusive — the investment returned nothing. What does 'illusive' mean?
options: Based on illusion — deceptive and not real
Appealing in a way that turned out to be unfounded
Too good to be true in precisely the way the warning suggested
Dependent on conditions that never actually materialized
quiz.answer: Based on illusion — deceptive and not real
quiz.explanation:
Illusive means illusory — based on illusion, deceptive or unreal.
• illusive = not real; based on illusion
• From Latin: 'illudere' — to mock
• Often confused with 'elusive' (hard to catch); 'illusive' = not what it appears

quiz.question: The prose style was so rebarbative that few readers persisted past the first chapter. What does 'rebarbative' mean?
options: Repellent and unattractive — off-putting in manner
Dense with jargon that made it impenetrable
Written with a difficulty that seemed deliberately hostile
Too technical for non-specialist readers to engage with
quiz.answer: Repellent and unattractive — off-putting in manner
quiz.explanation:
Rebarbative means unattractive and repellent — causing aversion.
• rebarbative = unattractive; tending to repel
• From French: 'rebarbatif' — grim-faced
• A rare but precise word for something that pushes people away

quiz.question: The results were indicative of a wider problem in the organization. What does 'indicative' mean?
options: Suggesting or pointing toward something
Supporting a conclusion that extended beyond the immediate data
Consistent with a pattern seen across the organization
Providing evidence for a hypothesis that warranted investigation
quiz.answer: Suggesting or pointing toward something
quiz.explanation:
Indicative means pointing to — suggesting or serving as a sign of.
• indicative = suggesting; serving as an indication of
• From Latin: 'indicare' — to point out
• 'Indicative of a trend', 'indicative vote' — pointing toward or signaling

quiz.question: The smell of old books was evocative of her childhood library. What does 'evocative' mean?
options: Bringing strong images or memories to mind
Associated so closely with a place that it produced vivid recall
Strong enough as a sensory trigger to produce a clear memory
Capable of transporting someone back to a specific time
quiz.answer: Bringing strong images or memories to mind
quiz.explanation:
Evocative means calling up memories or feelings — powerfully suggestive.
• evocative = tending to evoke; bringing memories vividly to mind
• From Latin: 'evocare' — to call out
• 'Evocative music', 'evocative description' — stirring feelings or memories

quiz.question: The doctor prescribed a mild sedative to help her sleep. What is a sedative?
options: A drug that induces calm or sleep
A medication given to reduce anxiety before a procedure
A prescribed substance that slows the nervous system
A sleep aid used to treat insomnia
quiz.answer: A drug that induces calm or sleep
quiz.explanation:
A sedative is a calming drug — one that reduces anxiety or induces sleep.
• sedative = a drug that sedates; tending to calm or produce sleep
• From Latin: 'sedare' — to calm
• 'Sedative effect', 'sedated patient' — calmed by medication

quiz.question: It is the director's prerogative to make the final decision. What is a prerogative?
options: A special right or privilege belonging to a particular person
The authority that comes with a specific position
A power that was formally granted to the role
A right exercised exclusively by those at a certain level, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: A special right or privilege belonging to a particular person
quiz.explanation:
A prerogative is an exclusive right — a privilege that belongs to a specific role.
• prerogative = a right or privilege exclusive to a particular person
• From Latin: 'praerogativa' — first vote
• 'Royal prerogative', 'it's my prerogative' — a right that cannot be disputed

quiz.question: The detective's interrogative style wore down the suspect's resistance over time. What does 'interrogative' mean?
options: Relating to questioning — involving or resembling interrogation
Persistent in asking questions and not accepting vague answers, as the evidence showed
Designed to extract information through sustained questioning
Structured to put the person being questioned under pressure
quiz.answer: Relating to questioning — involving or resembling interrogation
quiz.explanation:
Interrogative means relating to questioning — of or forming a question.
• interrogative = relating to questioning; a question
• From Latin: 'interrogare' — to question
• 'Interrogative sentence' = a question; 'interrogative manner' = questioning

quiz.question: In ancient medicine, a purgative was often prescribed to cleanse the body. What is a purgative?
options: A substance that causes the bowels to empty — a laxative
A treatment used to remove harmful substances from the body
A medical intervention designed to purge toxins
A preparation that cleared the digestive system
quiz.answer: A substance that causes the bowels to empty — a laxative
quiz.explanation:
A purgative is a strong laxative — a substance that purges the intestines.
• purgative = a laxative; tending to purge
• From Latin: 'purgare' — to purge
• Also figurative: 'a purgative experience' = one that clears and cleanses

quiz.question: The measures were merely palliative — they reduced the pain but did not treat the cause. What does 'palliative' mean?
options: Providing relief without addressing the underlying cause
Reducing suffering without offering any prospect of cure
Helpful in the short term but not solving the real problem
Designed to ease discomfort when no cure was available
quiz.answer: Providing relief without addressing the underlying cause
quiz.explanation:
Palliative means relieving without curing — easing but not treating.
• palliative = relieving pain without dealing with the cause
• From Latin: 'palliare' — to cover
• 'Palliative care' = care focused on comfort; 'palliative measure' = a temporary fix

quiz.question: The charity launched a new initiative to combat food waste in schools. What is an initiative?
options: A new plan or action to address a specific problem
A project organized to draw attention to a social issue
A structured program designed to achieve a defined goal
An effort launched to create change in a specific area
quiz.answer: A new plan or action to address a specific problem
quiz.explanation:
An initiative is a fresh plan or action — a scheme to achieve a specific goal.
• initiative = a new scheme or action; the ability to act without prompting
• From Latin: 'initiare' — to begin
• 'Take the initiative' = act first; 'launch an initiative' = start a new effort

quiz.question: He described her performance in superlatives — no word of praise was strong enough. What does 'superlative' mean?
options: Of the highest quality — expressing the most extreme degree
So impressive that ordinary language could not do it justice
Worthy of the highest possible praise from any audience
At a level beyond what even the most favorable reviews implied
quiz.answer: Of the highest quality — expressing the most extreme degree
quiz.explanation:
Superlative means of the highest degree — expressing the maximum quality.
• superlative = of the highest quality; expressing the greatest degree
• From Latin: 'superlatus' — carried above
• In grammar: the superlative form (best, fastest) expresses the extreme

quiz.question: The journalist admitted the report was speculative — based on limited information. What does 'speculative' mean?
options: Based on conjecture rather than established fact
Published before all the relevant facts had been confirmed
Extrapolating from limited data to a broader conclusion
Drawing inferences that the available evidence could not support
quiz.answer: Based on conjecture rather than established fact
quiz.explanation:
Speculative means based on guesswork — not confirmed by evidence.
• speculative = based on speculation; uncertain
• From Latin: 'speculari' — to spy out
• 'Speculative investment', 'speculative claim' — not grounded in certainty

quiz.question: Her manipulative behavior — flattering when she needed something, cold when she didn't — was well-known. What does 'manipulative' mean?
options: Controlling others through clever and often dishonest means
Skilled at using relationships as tools to achieve personal goals
Showing affection strategically rather than genuinely
Using emotional intelligence in ways that exploited others
quiz.answer: Controlling others through clever and often dishonest means
quiz.explanation:
Manipulative means controlling through deception — using cleverness to exploit.
• manipulative = tending to control others for one's own benefit
• From Latin: 'manipulus' — a handful
• 'Manipulative tactics', 'emotionally manipulative' — using people as instruments

quiz.question: Her formative years were spent in a small village that shaped her values permanently. What does 'formative' mean?
options: Relating to the period that shapes character or development
Influential in producing the values one would carry into adulthood
Especially important because of the impressions formed then
Decisive in terms of the habits and preferences that would follow
quiz.answer: Relating to the period that shapes character or development
quiz.explanation:
Formative means shaping — relating to the period that forms one's character.
• formative = serving to form; of or relating to development
• From Latin: 'formare' — to shape
• 'Formative years', 'formative experience' — the experiences that shape us

quiz.question: He chose the less prestigious but more remunerative career path. What does 'remunerative' mean?
options: Providing a good financial return — well-paying
Offering greater financial rewards than the alternative
More profitable than a similar role at a rival firm
Paying enough to compensate for the lack of prestige
quiz.answer: Providing a good financial return — well-paying
quiz.explanation:
Remunerative means financially rewarding — providing good payment.
• remunerative = providing good pay; profitable
• From Latin: 'remunerari' — to repay
• 'A remunerative position', 'remunerative work' — paying well

quiz.question: It is imperative that the results be verified before publication. What does 'imperative' mean?
options: Of vital importance — absolutely essential
Required by professional standards in the relevant field
Non-negotiable given the potential consequences of error
Urgent enough that it could not be delayed or overlooked
quiz.answer: Of vital importance — absolutely essential
quiz.explanation:
Imperative means absolutely necessary — of vital, urgent importance.
• imperative = of vital importance; giving an authoritative command
• From Latin: 'imperare' — to command
• 'Imperative to act', 'the moral imperative' — the urgent, commanding necessity

quiz.question: The new regulations became operative from the first of the month. What does 'operative' mean?
options: In effect — functioning and active
Applicable to all parties covered by the regulations
Binding from the date specified in the official announcement
Enforced by the relevant regulatory body from that date
quiz.answer: In effect — functioning and active
quiz.explanation:
Operative means in effect — active and functioning.
• operative = functioning; in operation; the most important word
• From Latin: 'operari' — to work
• 'The operative word', 'operative from today' — the thing that matters; what is active

quiz.question: The suspect was cooperative and answered every question put to her. What does 'cooperative' mean?
options: Willing to work with others — helpful and compliant
Forthcoming with information despite having no obligation to speak
Providing assistance that went beyond what was required
Demonstrating good faith by responding fully to all questions
quiz.answer: Willing to work with others — helpful and compliant
quiz.explanation:
Cooperative means willing to work together — helpful, compliant, and collaborative.
• cooperative = inclined to cooperate; involving joint effort
• From Latin: 'cooperari' — to work together
• 'A cooperative witness', 'cooperative approach' — working together willingly

quiz.question: The witness was uncooperative and refused to answer even simple questions. What does 'uncooperative' mean?
options: Unwilling to help or work with others
Resistant to questioning despite a legal obligation to answer
Failing to provide information that was clearly available
Obstructive in a way that delayed the process
quiz.answer: Unwilling to help or work with others
quiz.explanation:
Uncooperative means unwilling to work together — obstructive or unhelpful.
• uncooperative = not willing to cooperate
• Opposite of cooperative
• 'Uncooperative witness', 'uncooperative attitude' — refusing to help

quiz.question: The candidate's vituperative attacks on his opponent shocked even his supporters. What does 'vituperative' mean?
options: Bitterly and violently abusive in language
More aggressive in tone than any political attack they had seen
Designed to destroy rather than simply criticize
Aimed at character rather than policy in a sustained way
quiz.answer: Bitterly and violently abusive in language
quiz.explanation:
Vituperative means savagely abusive — characterized by harsh, bitter criticism.
• vituperative = filled with bitter and violent abuse
• From Latin: 'vituperare' — to find fault with
• 'A vituperative speech', 'vituperative criticism' — vicious verbal attack

quiz.question: The term was used in a pejorative sense — as an insult, not a neutral descriptor. What does 'pejorative' mean?
options: Expressing disapproval or contempt — disparaging
Negative in connotation regardless of its literal meaning
Intended to hurt or demean the person it was applied to
Carrying a meaning that the person being described resented
quiz.answer: Expressing disapproval or contempt — disparaging
quiz.explanation:
Pejorative means contemptuous — used to express disapproval or insult.
• pejorative = expressing contempt or disapproval
• From Latin: 'pejorare' — to make worse
• 'A pejorative term', 'used pejoratively' — used to put someone down

quiz.question: The president's team worked hard to control the narrative around the scandal. What is a narrative?
options: The story or account that shapes how something is understood
The version of events that the media would accept and repeat
The framework through which the public would interpret events
The message that the team wanted to be the dominant one
quiz.answer: The story or account that shapes how something is understood
quiz.explanation:
A narrative is a story or account — the structured way in which events are told.
• narrative = an account or story; the way events are presented
• From Latin: 'narrare' — to tell
• 'Control the narrative' = determine how events are interpreted and told

quiz.question: She was naturally demonstrative — hugging friends and expressing feelings openly. What does 'demonstrative' mean?
options: Openly expressive of feeling — showing emotions freely
Affectionate in a way that some found excessive
Warm in a way that left no one in doubt about how she felt
Expressive of emotion without concern for others' reactions
quiz.answer: Openly expressive of feeling — showing emotions freely
quiz.explanation:
Demonstrative means openly expressive — showing feelings without restraint.
• demonstrative = tending to show feelings openly
• From Latin: 'demonstrare' — to show
• 'A demonstrative greeting', 'demonstratively affectionate' — openly expressed

quiz.question: In the figurative sense, 'burning the candle at both ends' means working too hard. What does 'figurative' mean?
options: Used in a non-literal sense — metaphorical
Expressing an idea through a comparison rather than direct statement
Applied to something it does not literally describe
Meaning something other than the plain meaning of the words
quiz.answer: Used in a non-literal sense — metaphorical
quiz.explanation:
Figurative means metaphorical — using language to describe something other than what is literally meant.
• figurative = metaphorical; not literal
• From Latin: 'figura' — a form, figure
• 'Figurative language', 'in the figurative sense' — meaning beyond the literal

quiz.question: She walked the cliffs in a meditative state, thinking through the problem slowly. What does 'meditative' mean?
options: Relating to or characterized by quiet, reflective thought
Calm and focused in a way that excluded external distraction
Using the physical act of walking to aid reflective thinking
Turning a problem over in the mind without urgency
quiz.answer: Relating to or characterized by quiet, reflective thought
quiz.explanation:
Meditative means deeply reflective — engaged in quiet, focused contemplation.
• meditative = of or relating to meditation; deeply thoughtful
• From Latin: 'meditari' — to reflect
• 'A meditative walk', 'meditative mood' — quiet, inward, reflective

quiz.question: The child's imitative behavior showed she was learning language from those around her. What does 'imitative' mean?
options: Relating to or marked by imitation
Developing language by copying what was heard around her
Learning through observation and reproduction
Based on modeling the behavior of those in the environment
quiz.answer: Relating to or marked by imitation
quiz.explanation:
Imitative means involving imitation — copying as a mode of behavior or learning.
• imitative = marked by or given to imitation
• From Latin: 'imitari' — to copy
• 'Imitative learning', 'imitative art' — copying as the basis of the activity

quiz.question: The speech had a hortative tone — urging the audience to take immediate action. What does 'hortative' mean?
options: Tending to urge or encourage action
Delivered in a way designed to produce a strong response
Appealing to shared values to motivate a collective effort
Building toward a call to action through persuasion
quiz.answer: Tending to urge or encourage action
quiz.explanation:
Hortative means urging or encouraging — aimed at persuading someone to act.
• hortative = tending to urge or encourage
• From Latin: 'hortari' — to exhort
• 'A hortative passage', 'hortative rhetoric' — designed to move people to act

quiz.question: The putative author of the letter has never been conclusively identified. What does 'putative' mean?
options: Generally supposed or reputed to be — assumed
Identified by historians as the most likely candidate
Named in contemporary documents without being confirmed
Accepted by most authorities pending further research
quiz.answer: Generally supposed or reputed to be — assumed
quiz.explanation:
Putative means reputed or supposed — generally accepted but not definitively proven.
• putative = generally considered or reputed to be
• From Latin: 'putare' — to think
• 'The putative father', 'putative origin' — assumed but not proven

quiz.question: Critics dismissed the album as derivative — closely imitating artists who had come before. What does 'derivative' mean?
options: Lacking originality — copied or derived from something else
Inferior to the sources it drew on too heavily
Too obviously indebted to earlier artists to be interesting, as the evidence showed
Failing to transform its influences into something new
quiz.answer: Lacking originality — copied or derived from something else
quiz.explanation:
Derivative means lacking originality — based too closely on something else.
• derivative = derived from something; lacking originality
• From Latin: 'derivare' — to lead away
• 'Derivative work', 'derivative art' — not original

quiz.question: The company built its reputation on innovative design that nobody else had tried. What does 'innovative' mean?
options: Introducing new and original ideas — creative and inventive
Developing products that solved problems nobody had addressed
Ahead of the market in both concept and execution
Taking creative risks that produced commercially successful results
quiz.answer: Introducing new and original ideas — creative and inventive
quiz.explanation:
Innovative means introducing something new — creatively original.
• innovative = featuring new methods or ideas; creative
• From Latin: 'innovare' — to renew
• 'Innovative solution', 'innovative design' — introducing what is new

quiz.question: The food contained no artificial preservatives. What is a preservative?
options: A substance added to food to prevent decay
A chemical that extends the shelf life of food products
An additive that prevents microbial growth in food
A natural or artificial ingredient that slows spoilage
quiz.answer: A substance added to food to prevent decay
quiz.explanation:
A preservative is a substance that prevents decay — added to extend shelf life.
• preservative = a substance that preserves against decay
• From Latin: 'preservare' — to keep
• 'No artificial preservatives', 'preservative-free' — nothing added to prevent spoilage

quiz.question: He made a conservative estimate — deliberately choosing the lower end to avoid overpromising. What does 'conservative' mean?
options: Cautious and tending toward understatement
Calculated to avoid the risk of setting expectations too high
Lower than a more optimistic analysis might have suggested
Prudent enough to acknowledge uncertainty in the inputs
quiz.answer: Cautious and tending toward understatement
quiz.explanation:
Conservative means cautious — disposed to caution, avoiding overstatement.
• conservative = cautious; averse to change; tending to underestimate
• From Latin: 'conservare' — to keep
• 'A conservative estimate' = a deliberately low figure; 'conservative dress' = traditional

quiz.question: The doctor recommended a mild laxative to relieve the constipation. What is a laxative?
options: A medicine that relieves constipation by stimulating the bowels
A preparation that softens the stool to ease its passage, as the evidence showed
A medical treatment for a digestive problem
A drug given to normalize bowel function
quiz.answer: A medicine that relieves constipation by stimulating the bowels
quiz.explanation:
A laxative is a medicine that relieves constipation — causing bowel movement.
• laxative = a medicine that loosens the bowels
• From Latin: 'laxare' — to loosen
• 'Mild laxative', 'bulk-forming laxative' — different types of bowel loosener

quiz.question: The scientists used a nonradioactive tracer to track the pathway. What does 'nonradioactive' mean?
options: Not emitting radiation — safe from radioactive decay
Designed to be used in environments where radiation is hazardous
Producing the same results as a radioactive tracer without the risk
A form of tracer that does not require protective equipment
quiz.answer: Not emitting radiation — safe from radioactive decay
quiz.explanation:
Nonradioactive means not radioactive — not undergoing or emitting radiation.
• nonradioactive = not exhibiting radioactivity
• From 'non-' + 'radioactive'
• 'Nonradioactive isotope', 'nonradioactive tracer' — safe from radiation

quiz.question: The pay increase was retroactive to the start of the year. What does 'retroactive' mean?
options: Taking effect from a point in the past — applying retrospectively
Backdated to an earlier date than its announcement
Applied to a period before the decision was formally made
Extended to cover the full year despite being approved midway, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: Taking effect from a point in the past — applying retrospectively
quiz.explanation:
Retroactive means effective from a past date — applying backwards in time.
• retroactive = taking effect from a date in the past
• From Latin: 'retro-' (backward) + 'activus'
• 'Retroactive legislation', 'retroactive pay rise' — backdated effect

quiz.question: The investigation required an objective assessment, free from personal bias. What does 'objective' mean?
options: Not influenced by personal feelings — factual and impartial
Based on evidence rather than on assumptions or preferences
Conducted in a way that no reasonable person could criticize
Applying the same standards to all parties regardless of relationship
quiz.answer: Not influenced by personal feelings — factual and impartial
quiz.explanation:
Objective means impartial — not influenced by personal feelings or bias.
• objective = not influenced by feelings; factual
• From Latin: 'objectivus' — of or relating to an object
• Opposite: subjective; 'objective assessment' = based on facts, not feelings

quiz.question: Beauty is subjective — what one person finds beautiful, another may find dull. What does 'subjective' mean?
options: Based on personal feelings or opinion rather than fact
Varying from person to person depending on individual taste
Not capable of being measured by any objective standard
Determined by perception rather than by any external criterion
quiz.answer: Based on personal feelings or opinion rather than fact
quiz.explanation:
Subjective means based on personal viewpoint — influenced by individual feelings.
• subjective = influenced by personal feelings; relating to the subject
• Opposite of objective
• 'Subjective experience', 'subjective judgment' — varies by individual

quiz.question: She became more introspective after the experience, spending hours alone with her thoughts. What does 'introspective' mean?
options: Given to looking inward — examining one's own thoughts and feelings
Withdrawn from social life as a result of a difficult experience, as the evidence showed
More interested in her internal world than in external events
Spending time processing emotions rather than expressing them
quiz.answer: Given to looking inward — examining one's own thoughts and feelings
quiz.explanation:
Introspective means self-examining — tending to look inward.
• introspective = examining one's own mind and feelings
• From Latin: 'introspicere' — to look within
• 'An introspective person', 'introspective writing' — focused inward

quiz.question: It is important to keep things in perspective during difficult times. What does 'keep in perspective' mean?
options: To see something in relation to the bigger picture — proportionately
To avoid overreacting to difficulties by remembering they are temporary
To maintain a sense of proportion about one's own problems
To relate current difficulties to the larger context of life
quiz.answer: To see something in relation to the bigger picture — proportionately
quiz.explanation:
Perspective means a proportionate view — seeing something in its proper relation to everything else.
• perspective = a particular way of viewing; proportion
• From Latin: 'perspicere' — to see through
• 'Keep in perspective', 'from my perspective' — relative view

quiz.question: The speech was a torrent of invective against every aspect of the government's record. What is invective?
options: Insulting or abusive language — violent verbal attack
A sustained public attack on the record of those in power
Political criticism delivered in the most extreme terms available
A speech focused entirely on attacking rather than proposing
quiz.answer: Insulting or abusive language — violent verbal attack
quiz.explanation:
Invective is violent, abusive language — bitter denunciation.
• invective = insulting language; a violent verbal attack
• From Latin: 'invehi' — to attack
• 'A torrent of invective', 'vitriolic invective' — relentless verbal assault

quiz.question: The twin spires gave the cathedral a highly distinctive silhouette. What does 'distinctive' mean?
options: Characteristic and recognizable — easily distinguished
Unusual enough to attract attention from visitors
Architectural in a way that had become associated with the building
Prominent enough to be visible from a great distance
quiz.answer: Characteristic and recognizable — easily distinguished
quiz.explanation:
Distinctive means characteristic and recognizable — setting something apart clearly.
• distinctive = characteristic; distinguishing from others
• From Latin: 'distinctus' — separated
• 'A distinctive voice', 'distinctive style' — unmistakably itself

quiz.question: Her instinctive reaction was to step back before she knew what she had seen. What does 'instinctive' mean?
options: Acting without conscious thought — prompted by instinct
Reacting physically before the mind had processed what was there
Produced by the body's defensive systems before awareness
Automatic in a way that bypassed rational deliberation
quiz.answer: Acting without conscious thought — prompted by instinct
quiz.explanation:
Instinctive means prompted by instinct — acting without conscious deliberation.
• instinctive = prompted by natural instinct; done without thinking
• From Latin: 'instinctus' — impulse
• 'Instinctive response', 'instinctive dislike' — spontaneous, not deliberate

quiz.question: The committee faced a disjunctive choice — either cut spending or raise taxes, but not both. What does 'disjunctive' mean?
options: Involving a choice between alternatives that exclude each other
Presenting two options of which only one could be selected
Requiring a decision between incompatible courses of action, as the evidence showed
Forcing a binary choice where compromise was not possible
quiz.answer: Involving a choice between alternatives that exclude each other
quiz.explanation:
Disjunctive means presenting mutually exclusive alternatives — either/or.
• disjunctive = expressing alternatives that are mutually exclusive
• From Latin: 'disjungere' — to separate
• 'A disjunctive choice' = one where choosing one option excludes the other

quiz.question: Using deductive reasoning, he concluded that only one person could have been responsible. What does 'deductive' mean?
options: Drawing specific conclusions from general principles
Working from what is known to determine what must be true
Eliminating possibilities until only one explanation remained
Applying logical principles to reach a definitive conclusion
quiz.answer: Drawing specific conclusions from general principles
quiz.explanation:
Deductive means working from general principles to specific conclusions.
• deductive = proceeding from the general to the particular
• From Latin: 'deducere' — to lead down
• 'Deductive reasoning' starts with a general rule and applies it to a case

quiz.question: The meeting was entirely unproductive — nothing was decided and no action was agreed. What does 'unproductive' mean?
options: Failing to achieve any useful result
Taking up time without producing any of the expected outcomes
Wasting the participants' time because of poor organization
Covering none of the items that were on the original agenda
quiz.answer: Failing to achieve any useful result
quiz.explanation:
Unproductive means failing to produce — yielding no useful outcome.
• unproductive = not producing results
• Opposite of productive
• 'Unproductive meeting', 'unproductive land' — failing to produce

quiz.question: Increased regulation, instead of helping, proved counterproductive and stifled innovation. What does 'counterproductive' mean?
options: Having the opposite effect to the one intended
Making the problem worse rather than better through intervention
Producing outcomes that directly contradicted the policy's goals
Applying a solution that aggravated rather than resolved the issue
quiz.answer: Having the opposite effect to the one intended
quiz.explanation:
Counterproductive means achieving the opposite of the intended effect.
• counterproductive = having the opposite of the desired effect
• From 'counter-' + 'productive'
• 'Counterproductive strategy' = the intervention makes things worse

quiz.question: The failure was instructive — it revealed exactly where the process had broken down. What does 'instructive' mean?
options: Providing useful information or a lesson
Revealing in a way that made the cause of failure clear
Detailed enough to allow the same mistake to be avoided
Producing insights that would inform future decisions
quiz.answer: Providing useful information or a lesson
quiz.explanation:
Instructive means informative and educational — providing a useful lesson.
• instructive = serving to instruct; informative
• From Latin: 'instruere' — to build, instruct
• 'An instructive example', 'instructive failure' — teaching something valuable

quiz.question: The cost of treatment was prohibitive for most patients. What does 'prohibitive' mean?
options: So high as to prevent access — effectively barring something
More expensive than the majority of people could afford
So costly that only the wealthiest could consider it
Priced at a level that placed it beyond most people's reach, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: So high as to prevent access — effectively barring something
quiz.explanation:
Prohibitive means so extreme as to prohibit — barring through cost or restriction.
• prohibitive = too high or severe to be practicable
• From Latin: 'prohibere' — to prevent
• 'Prohibitive cost', 'prohibitive tariff' — so extreme it stops people from proceeding

quiz.question: The food manufacturer listed all additives on the label. What is an additive?
options: A substance added to something to improve or preserve it
A chemical included in processed food to enhance flavor
A listed ingredient that was not part of the original recipe
A compound added during manufacturing rather than cooking
quiz.answer: A substance added to something to improve or preserve it
quiz.explanation:
An additive is something added — especially to food for preservation or enhancement.
• additive = a substance added to improve or preserve
• From Latin: 'addere' — to add
• 'Food additive', 'additive-free' — added to achieve a specific effect

quiz.question: She wrote the definitive biography — the one all subsequent authors referred to. What does 'definitive' mean?
options: Authoritative and final — the most complete treatment of its kind
Comprehensive enough to make further biographies unnecessary
So well-researched that its conclusions could not be challenged, as the evidence showed
The best available work in its field at the time of publication
quiz.answer: Authoritative and final — the most complete treatment of its kind
quiz.explanation:
Definitive means authoritative and conclusive — the ultimate version of something.
• definitive = most authoritative; conclusive
• From Latin: 'definire' — to determine
• 'The definitive edition', 'definitive answer' — the final, authoritative word

quiz.question: He was acquisitive by nature — always seeking to add to his collection. What does 'acquisitive' mean?
options: Strongly motivated by the desire to acquire and possess
Driven by a need to accumulate more than one needed
Finding satisfaction in owning rather than in using
Unable to resist adding new items to an existing collection
quiz.answer: Strongly motivated by the desire to acquire and possess
quiz.explanation:
Acquisitive means eager to acquire — strongly motivated by the desire to possess.
• acquisitive = excessively interested in acquiring things
• From Latin: 'acquirere' — to acquire
• 'An acquisitive nature', 'acquisitive instinct' — the drive to gather and own

quiz.question: The inquisitive child asked questions about everything she encountered. What does 'inquisitive' mean?
options: Curious and eager to learn by questioning
Interested enough in her surroundings to ask about them
Driven by a desire to understand the world through asking
Unable to accept what she saw without wanting to know more
quiz.answer: Curious and eager to learn by questioning
quiz.explanation:
Inquisitive means curious — eager to learn through questioning and investigating.
• inquisitive = curious; eager to inquire
• From Latin: 'inquirere' — to seek after
• 'An inquisitive mind', 'inquisitive child' — driven by curiosity

quiz.question: The diplomatic matter required a sensitive approach. What does 'sensitive' mean?
options: Requiring careful handling — easily affected or complicated
Of a nature that could cause damage if handled carelessly, as the evidence showed
Too charged emotionally for a direct approach to work
Likely to produce a strong reaction if mishandled
quiz.answer: Requiring careful handling — easily affected or complicated
quiz.explanation:
Sensitive means easily affected — requiring careful handling.
• sensitive = responding readily; easily offended; requiring care
• From Latin: 'sentire' — to feel
• 'Sensitive topic', 'sensitive instrument' — easily affected

quiz.question: The interface was praised for its intuitive design — users understood it immediately. What does 'intuitive' mean?
options: Understood instinctively — not requiring explanation
Simple enough that any user could navigate it without training
Logical enough that new users found it immediately familiar
Designed to match the expectations of a typical user
quiz.answer: Understood instinctively — not requiring explanation
quiz.explanation:
Intuitive means immediately understood — grasped through intuition rather than instruction.
• intuitive = using intuition rather than conscious reasoning
• From Latin: 'intueri' — to look at
• 'Intuitive design', 'intuitive leap' — understanding without needing to be told

quiz.question: The talks produced substantive progress — three major issues were resolved. What does 'substantive' mean?
options: Having real importance or substance — meaningful
Involving enough progress to justify the time spent
Resulting in outcomes that could be reported as concrete
Going beyond symbolic gestures to produce real change
quiz.answer: Having real importance or substance — meaningful
quiz.explanation:
Substantive means having real substance — significant and meaningful.
• substantive = real and important; having substance
• From Latin: 'substantivus' — standing by itself
• 'Substantive change', 'substantive discussion' — not superficial

quiz.question: Tax breaks were offered as an incentive for businesses to relocate. What is an incentive?
options: Something that motivates or encourages a specific action
A financial benefit designed to alter behavior
A reward that made a particular course of action more attractive
A benefit offered to produce the desired economic outcome
quiz.answer: Something that motivates or encourages a specific action
quiz.explanation:
An incentive is a motivating factor — something that encourages a particular action.
• incentive = something that incites or motivates action
• From Latin: 'incentivus' — setting the tune
• 'Financial incentive', 'incentivize behavior' — motivating through reward

quiz.question: She had a retentive memory that meant she never forgot a name or a face. What does 'retentive' mean?
options: Having the ability to retain information well
Able to recall in detail what had been learned or observed
Capable of storing a large volume of information accurately
Not prone to forgetting things that others would overlook
quiz.answer: Having the ability to retain information well
quiz.explanation:
Retentive means good at retaining — capable of holding information in memory.
• retentive = tending to retain; having a good memory
• From Latin: 'retinere' — to hold back
• 'A retentive memory', 'retentive mind' — good at keeping what is learned

quiz.question: A plaintive cry came from the abandoned building late at night. What does 'plaintive' mean?
options: Expressing sorrow or longing — mournfully sad
Audible enough to be heard but too faint to identify its source
Coming from somewhere inside in a way that suggested distress
Repeated at intervals in a way that increased the sense of unease
quiz.answer: Expressing sorrow or longing — mournfully sad
quiz.explanation:
Plaintive means sorrowful — expressing sadness or longing.
• plaintive = mournful; expressing sorrow
• From Old French: 'plaintif' — lamenting
• 'A plaintive melody', 'a plaintive cry' — conveying sadness

quiz.question: The steam locomotive pulling the vintage train was over a hundred years old. What is a locomotive?
options: A powered vehicle that pulls a train along rails
The engine car at the front of a passenger train
A steam-powered vehicle that preceded diesel and electric trains
The driving unit of a train that provides its motive power
quiz.answer: A powered vehicle that pulls a train along rails
quiz.explanation:
A locomotive is a railway engine — the powered unit that moves a train.
• locomotive = a self-propelled vehicle that moves a railway train
• From Latin: 'loco' (from a place) + 'motivus' (of motion)
• 'Steam locomotive', 'locomotive power' — the engine that drives the train

quiz.question: She was receptive to new ideas and always willing to consider alternative approaches. What does 'receptive' mean?
options: Open and willing to receive new ideas or suggestions
Interested in ideas that challenged her existing assumptions
Prepared to change her mind when given good reasons
Responsive to input from colleagues regardless of their seniority
quiz.answer: Open and willing to receive new ideas or suggestions
quiz.explanation:
Receptive means open to receiving — willing to accept new ideas or influence.
• receptive = open to new ideas; willing to receive
• From Latin: 'receptivus' — able to receive
• 'Receptive audience', 'receptive to change' — open and willing

quiz.question: Critics called the film's ending redemptive — the protagonist found meaning after suffering. What does 'redemptive' mean?
options: Saving or restoring — having the quality of redemption
Providing a resolution that compensated for earlier pain
Offering a positive conclusion that justified the difficulty
Allowing a character to find peace after a period of suffering
quiz.answer: Saving or restoring — having the quality of redemption
quiz.explanation:
Redemptive means saving or restoring — bringing redemption or a positive transformation.
• redemptive = serving to redeem; saving from failure or error
• From Latin: 'redimere' — to buy back
• 'A redemptive arc', 'redemptive ending' — character is saved or restored

quiz.question: The new technology was highly disruptive to the traditional industry. What does 'disruptive' mean?
options: Causing disruption — breaking up established patterns
Introducing such rapid change that existing businesses struggled
Forcing the industry to adapt faster than it could manage
Creating instability in a sector that had previously been stable
quiz.answer: Causing disruption — breaking up established patterns
quiz.explanation:
Disruptive means causing disruption — breaking apart what was established.
• disruptive = causing disturbance; breaking up
• From Latin: 'disrumpere' — to break apart
• 'Disruptive technology', 'disruptive student' — breaking the pattern

quiz.question: The training helped her become more assertive in meetings — stating her views clearly. What does 'assertive' mean?
options: Confident in stating one's position — self-assured
Willing to speak even when others were louder
Able to express opinions without being pushed aside
Direct in a way that earned the respect of her colleagues
quiz.answer: Confident in stating one's position — self-assured
quiz.explanation:
Assertive means confident and direct — willing to state one's position clearly.
• assertive = having or showing confidence; self-assured
• From Latin: 'asserere' — to claim
• 'Assertive communication', 'assertive manner' — clear and direct

quiz.question: The abortive coup attempt left the conspirators in prison and the government strengthened. What does 'abortive' mean?
options: Failing to succeed — ending prematurely without achieving its aim
Organized without the planning needed to have any chance, as the evidence showed
Suppressed so quickly that no lasting damage was done
Failed in a way that made all the participants worse off
quiz.answer: Failing to succeed — ending prematurely without achieving its aim
quiz.explanation:
Abortive means failing before completion — coming to nothing.
• abortive = failing to succeed; ending prematurely
• From Latin: 'abortivus' — born prematurely
• 'Abortive attempt', 'abortive coup' — failed before achieving its purpose

quiz.question: The festive atmosphere in the market reflected the upcoming holiday. What does 'festive' mean?
options: Relating to a celebration — joyful and suitable for a festival
Created by traders who had decorated their stalls for the season
Lively because people were in a mood to spend and celebrate
Seasonal in a way that lifted the spirits of those shopping
quiz.answer: Relating to a celebration — joyful and suitable for a festival
quiz.explanation:
Festive means relating to a festival — cheerful and celebratory.
• festive = relating to or suitable for a festival; joyful
• From Latin: 'festivus' — of a feast
• 'Festive season', 'festive spirit' — celebratory and joyful

quiz.question: The evidence was suggestive but not conclusive — it pointed in one direction without proving it. What does 'suggestive' mean?
options: Hinting at or implying something — raising the idea without proving it
Providing enough evidence to warrant a more thorough investigation, as the evidence showed
Consistent with one hypothesis without ruling out others
Supporting a conclusion without being strong enough to confirm it
quiz.answer: Hinting at or implying something — raising the idea without proving it
quiz.explanation:
Suggestive means implying or hinting — raising a possibility without confirming.
• suggestive = tending to suggest; evocative of something
• From Latin: 'suggerere' — to supply
• 'Suggestive evidence', 'suggestive remarks' — hinting without stating directly

quiz.question: The crowd grew restive as the speeches dragged on past midnight. What does 'restive' mean?
options: Restless and impatient — difficult to control
Increasingly vocal about wanting the event to end
Tired enough that concentration was no longer possible
Ready to leave but remaining out of politeness
quiz.answer: Restless and impatient — difficult to control
quiz.explanation:
Restive means restless and resistant — impatient and hard to keep still.
• restive = unable to keep still; impatient
• From Old French: 'restif' — stubborn
• 'A restive horse', 'a restive crowd' — not content to remain still

quiz.question: The exhaustive investigation covered every aspect of the company's operations. What does 'exhaustive' mean?
options: Extremely thorough — leaving nothing out
Conducted over such a long period that no stone was left unturned
Covering every possible angle of the subject under review
Producing so much documentation that it was difficult to digest
quiz.answer: Extremely thorough — leaving nothing out
quiz.explanation:
Exhaustive means completely thorough — covering every possible aspect.
• exhaustive = including every possible item; very thorough
• From Latin: 'exhaurire' — to drain dry
• 'Exhaustive search', 'exhaustive study' — nothing left out

quiz.question: She applied a soothing salve to the burn. What is a salve?
options: A healing ointment applied to wounds or sores
A medicated cream used to treat skin conditions
A preparation that reduces pain and promotes healing
A topical treatment applied directly to damaged skin
quiz.answer: A healing ointment applied to wounds or sores
quiz.explanation:
A salve is a soothing ointment — a thick healing cream applied to skin.
• salve = a healing ointment; something that soothes
• From Old English: 'sealf' — ointment
• 'Lip salve', 'salve for the conscience' — soothing, literally or figuratively

quiz.question: The engineer replaced a faulty valve in the pipeline. What is a valve?
options: A device that controls the flow of a fluid through a passage
A mechanical component that regulates pressure in a system, as the evidence showed
A fitting that allows liquid to pass in one direction only
A component that can be opened or closed to control flow
quiz.answer: A device that controls the flow of a fluid through a passage
quiz.explanation:
A valve is a flow-control device — regulating the passage of fluids.
• valve = a device for controlling the passage of fluid
• From Latin: 'valva' — a leaf of a door
• 'Heart valve', 'safety valve', 'valve in a pipe' — controlling flow

quiz.question: The axe blade was fitted with a new helve after the old one snapped. What is a helve?
options: The handle of an axe or similar tool
The wooden shaft that provides grip on a hand tool
The part of a tool held when it is being used
The replaceable wooden component of a chopping tool
quiz.answer: The handle of an axe or similar tool
quiz.explanation:
A helve is a handle — the shaft of an axe, hatchet, or hammer.
• helve = the handle of an axe, hatchet, or other tool
• From Old English: 'helfe' — a handle
• 'A new helve for the axe' — the wooden handle

quiz.question: The priest absolved him of his sins after confession. What does 'absolve' mean?
options: To formally release from guilt, obligation, or blame
To grant forgiveness following a confession of wrongdoing
To declare someone free from sin through a religious rite
To use spiritual authority to remove the guilt of an act
quiz.answer: To formally release from guilt, obligation, or blame
quiz.explanation:
To absolve means to formally free from blame — to declare not guilty.
• absolve = to declare free from guilt or obligation
• From Latin: 'absolvere' — to loosen from
• 'Absolved of guilt', 'absolve responsibility' — released from the burden

quiz.question: Power was devolved to regional governments under the reform. What does 'devolve' mean?
options: To transfer power or responsibility to a lower level
To give regional bodies the authority previously held centrally
To redistribute decision-making from the center to the regions
To grant elected assemblies the powers needed to govern locally
quiz.answer: To transfer power or responsibility to a lower level
quiz.explanation:
To devolve means to transfer downward — to hand power or responsibility to a lower authority.
• devolve = to transfer to another; to pass on
• From Latin: 'devolvere' — to roll down
• 'Devolution of power', 'devolve responsibility' — transferring downward

quiz.question: She had set up a reading nook in the alcove at the end of the hallway. What is an alcove?
options: A small recess in a wall — a sheltered space set back from a room
A nook created by partitioning off a section of a room
A corner space made private by the addition of shelving
A small area separated from the main room by a half-wall, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: A small recess in a wall — a sheltered space set back from a room
quiz.explanation:
An alcove is a recess — a small space set into a wall or set apart from a room.
• alcove = a recess in a wall; a small sheltered space
• From Arabic: 'al-qubba' — a vault
• 'A bedroom alcove', 'a garden alcove' — recessed or sheltered space

quiz.question: It behooves all citizens to be aware of their legal rights. What does 'behoove' mean?
options: To be the responsibility or duty of someone — to be fitting
To be expected of those who consider themselves informed
To be in one's best interest to know and understand
To be a basic requirement of anyone living under a legal system
quiz.answer: To be the responsibility or duty of someone — to be fitting
quiz.explanation:
To behoove means to be proper or necessary for — to be fitting.
• behoove = to be necessary or appropriate for
• From Old English: 'behofian' — to need
• 'It behooves you to...' = it is your duty or fitting for you to...

quiz.question: The team found their groove in the second half and played with confidence. What does 'groove' mean here?
options: An established rhythm or pattern of effective performance
The level at which a team performed at its highest potential
A style of play that felt natural and produced results
A period of sustained performance that raised their confidence
quiz.answer: An established rhythm or pattern of effective performance
quiz.explanation:
A groove (figuratively) is a productive rhythm — an established pattern of smooth performance.
• groove = a settled routine; a rhythmic pattern
• From Dutch: 'groeve' — a channel
• 'In the groove' = performing smoothly; 'groove' in music = the rhythmic feel

quiz.question: She roved through the market stalls without any particular destination in mind. What does 'rove' mean?
options: To wander freely without a fixed destination
To browse at leisure through what was on offer
To move through a crowded space at an unhurried pace
To drift from stall to stall without any specific purpose
quiz.answer: To wander freely without a fixed destination
quiz.explanation:
To rove means to wander — to move freely without a fixed direction.
• rove = to wander; to roam
• Origin uncertain; possibly from 'rover' — a pirate
• 'Rove through the countryside', 'roving reporter' — moving freely

quiz.question: A drove of cattle was being herded along the old track toward the market. What is a drove?
options: A large group of animals being moved together
A herd of cattle driven along a traditional route
Any livestock gathered for the purpose of sale
A column of cattle moving under the direction of herders
quiz.answer: A large group of animals being moved together
quiz.explanation:
A drove is a herd of animals being driven — especially cattle moving together.
• drove = a herd of animals being driven; a large number moving together
• From Old English: 'draf' — a driving
• 'Droves of tourists' = very large numbers; literally: a moving herd

quiz.question: They picnicked in a grove of ancient oak trees near the riverbank. What is a grove?
options: A small group of trees — a wooded area without undergrowth
A natural clearing surrounded by old trees
A shaded area formed by trees growing close together, as the evidence showed
Any wooded area suitable for rest and shelter
quiz.answer: A small group of trees — a wooded area without undergrowth
quiz.explanation:
A grove is a small cluster of trees — a wooded area, typically without thick undergrowth.
• grove = a small wood or group of trees
• From Old English: 'graf' — a grove
• 'An olive grove', 'a sacred grove' — a cluster of trees with a distinct character
